title: Providing for consideration of the joint resolution (H.J. Res. 24) providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Department of Energy relating to "Energy Conservation Program: Energy Conservation Standards for Walk-In Coolers and Walk-In Freezers"; providing for consideration of the joint resolution (H.J. Res. 75) providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, Department of Energy relating to "Energy Conservation Program: Energy Conservation Standards for Commercial Refrigerators, Freezers, and Refrigerator-Freezers"; and providing for consideration of the bill (H.R. 1048) to amend the Higher Education Act of 1965 to strengthen disclosure requirements relating to foreign gifts and contracts, to prohibit contracts between institutions of higher education and certain foreign entities and countries of concern, and for other purposes.
plain_summary: This resolution sets up the rules for the House of Representatives to vote on three different pieces of legislation. Two of the bills would cancel new energy rules from the Department of Energy that require walk-in coolers and freezers to use less energy. The third bill would make colleges report more information about money and contracts they get from foreign countries and would stop them from working with certain foreign groups that might be dangerous. The resolution just decides how these three bills will be discussed and voted on by Congress.
last_action_text: Motion to reconsider laid on the table Agreed to without objection.

sections.title: Section 1 Walk-In Cooler and Freezer Energy Rule
sections.text: When this resolution is approved, the House of Representatives can consider House Joint Resolution twenty-four. This joint resolution would reject a rule from the Department of Energy about energy conservation standards for walk-in coolers and walk-in freezers. The House will skip normal procedural rules that might prevent consideration of this resolution. The resolution will be treated as if it has already been read out loud. The House will skip other procedural rules that might block parts of the resolution. The House will move directly to a final vote with only two exceptions. First, there will be one hour of debate time split equally between the chairperson and ranking minority member of the Committee on Energy and Commerce, or people they choose to speak for them. Second, there can be one motion to recommit (which means to send the resolution back to committee for changes).

sections.title: Section 2 Commercial Refrigerator Energy Rule
sections.text: When this resolution is approved, the House of Representatives can consider House Joint Resolution seventy-five. This joint resolution would reject a rule from the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy at the Department of Energy about energy conservation standards for commercial refrigerators, freezers, and refrigerator-freezers. The House will skip normal procedural rules that might prevent consideration of this resolution. The resolution will be treated as if it has already been read out loud. The House will skip other procedural rules that might block parts of the resolution. The House will move directly to a final vote with only two exceptions. First, there will be one hour of debate time split equally between the chairperson and ranking minority member of the Committee on Energy and Commerce, or people they choose to speak for them. Second, there can be one motion to recommit (which means to send the resolution back to committee for changes).

sections.title: Section 3 Higher Education Foreign Contracts
sections.text: After this resolution is approved, the Speaker of the House can declare that the House will transform into the Committee of the Whole House to consider House bill one thousand forty-eight. This bill would change the Higher Education Act of nineteen sixty-five to make colleges and universities report more information about gifts and contracts from foreign sources. The bill would also ban contracts between colleges and universities and certain foreign groups and countries that cause concern. The House will skip reading the bill out loud the first time. The House will skip normal procedural rules that might prevent consideration of the bill. General debate about the bill will last no more than one hour, split equally between the chairperson and ranking minority member of the Committee on Education and Workforce, or people they choose to speak for them. After general debate, members can offer amendments to the bill under the five-minute rule (which limits speaking time). Instead of using the amendment that the Committee on Education and Workforce recommended, the House will use an amendment that matches the text in Rules Committee Print one hundred nineteen dash one. This new version will be treated as the starting point for further amendments. The House will skip procedural rules that might block parts of the amended bill. Only amendments that are printed in the Rules Committee report can be offered. These amendments must be offered in the order listed in the report and only by the person named in the report. Each amendment will be treated as read, can be debated for the time listed in the report with time split between a supporter and opponent, cannot be changed, and cannot be split into separate votes. The House will skip procedural rules that might block these amendments. When amendment consideration is finished, the Committee will report the bill back to the full House. The House will move directly to a final vote with only one exception for a motion to recommit (which means to send the bill back to committee for changes).
